# Questions in Livestream 80

Live Stream #80: Drs Falk and Kennedy Debate After Party

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=676" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:16</a> | Did Titus Kennedy knowingly misstate things, or was he misled by confirmation bias? | Falk says some points, such as the handling of strata and destruction layers, are basic enough that Kennedy should have known they were wrong. | Falk distinguishes ordinary mistakes from points he thinks Kennedy had no excuse to miss. His example is Hazor stratigraphy: an occupation layer ends with destruction rather than beginning with it, so treating strata 3 and 2 as Kennedy did is, in Falk's view, a basic archaeological error that a trained scholar should recognize. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=888" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:48</a> | How can a debater expose false claims without simply calling the other person a liar? | He says an interlocutor has to pick battles and expose the data problem directly, because the culture resists blunt accusations of lying. | Falk says the social setting makes direct accusations hard, so the practical method is to select concrete claims and show why the data cannot support them. He uses examples such as claims about Semitic tombs and agricultural leveling to illustrate exposing the falsehood through evidence rather than relying only on labels. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=1492" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:52</a> | Was the Falk-Kennedy event really a conversation rather than a debate? | Falk says it was intended as a discussion between scholars, but the topic is emotionally charged because the early-date position functions as a literalism test for many supporters. | Falk says he approached it as a scholar-to-scholar discussion and wanted to avoid pure combativeness. He adds that the topic becomes charged because some viewers treat the early Exodus date as a test of biblical literalism and therefore react as if chronology disagreements threaten their faith commitments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=1762" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:22</a> | Could Falk explain the low, middle, and high chronology discussion further? | He says computer-assisted chronology has changed the field, the high chronology no longer works, Assyrian middle chronology was a compromise, and Egyptian chronology is now closer to a middle-low or low chronology depending on the dynasty. | Falk explains that older chronological models were partly shaped by limited hand calculation, while modern computer-assisted testing can process many synchronisms at once. He rejects the high chronology, describes the Assyrian middle chronology as a compromise position, and says Egyptian dates now tend toward middle-low in Dynasty 18 and lower in Dynasty 19. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=2307" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:27</a> | Have early-date proponents stopped saying Avaris was abandoned under Amenhotep II and shifted to saying only the palatial district was abandoned? | Falk says Kennedy lowered the bar by speaking of upheaval rather than abandonment, because Bietak does not show that the whole city of Avaris was abandoned under Amenhotep II. | Falk says the earlier argument depended on Avaris being abandoned, but Kennedy's phrasing softened that to upheaval or a narrower palatial district claim. Falk stresses that Bietak's reports do not show citywide abandonment in Amenhotep II's reign, so moving to a smaller claim concedes too much of the original argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=2637" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:57</a> | Does Rabbi Hillel Hanasi's chronology stand as an argument if its Jerusalem destruction date is wrong? | Falk has little confidence in arguments based on Seder Olam alone; he thinks Lepsius may have reached the right ballpark, but ancient sources are poor guides to events long before their own time. | Falk says Hillel's chronology is late and therefore not strong evidence for much earlier events. He grants that Lepsius may have landed in a reasonable range, but he treats that as a better modern reconstruction rather than a vindication of Seder Olam as an independent chronological authority. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=3004" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:04</a> | How do relative chronological synchronisms get tied to absolute calendar dates? | He points to agreed anchor points such as the death of Josiah and Nekau II's invasion, says later dates are solidly chronicled, and argues that pre-664 BC dates rely on many synchronisms that need computer analysis. | Falk explains that secure later anchors, including Josiah's death and Nekau II's invasion, help tie relative sequences to calendar years. Before 664 BC, he says the evidence becomes a large web of cross-cultural synchronisms, so reliable dating depends on testing many interlocking points rather than a single isolated datum. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=3493" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:13</a> | Which dates for Ramesses II does Falk prefer, and why did Kitchen's dates shift? | He prefers 1288-1222 BC and says Kitchen moved to 1279 because of an extended 28-year reign for Horemheb, which Falk rejects because Horemheb's wine dockets support a shorter reign. | Falk says he follows a lower Ramesses II date range than Kitchen's later 1279 accession. The difference turns on Horemheb: Falk rejects a 28-year reign because the wine docket evidence points to a shorter reign, which in turn affects the placement of Ramesses II. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=3646" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:46</a> | Should Septuagint variants in Kings be taken into account for biblical chronology? | Falk generally leans toward the Masoretic text for the reigns of the kings, while acknowledging variants and depending on Thiele's later amended chronology with Kitchen's comments. | Falk acknowledges that textual variants matter, but he does not rebuild the chronology mainly from the Septuagint. He says he tends to rely on the Masoretic reign data, using Thiele's amended work and Kitchen's comments, while recognizing that the material is complicated and not solved by one textual witness alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=3923" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:23</a> | Could Shishak's attack have occurred in Rehoboam's first sole year after Solomon's death? | Falk thinks the proposal may create consistency problems with later co-regencies and successors, so it would need to be tested in the chronology calculations. | Falk does not rule out the question by assertion, but says the proposal has to be tested against the full regnal framework. Moving Shishak into Rehoboam's first sole year may affect co-regencies and the sequence after Rehoboam, so he treats it as a calculation problem rather than an easy fix. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=4132" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:52</a> | If Solomon had the land, plans, and contracts, why did he start building the temple only in his fourth year? | Falk says the text gives the fourth year, but the reason is unknown; a need to stabilize the kingdom or a short David-Solomon co-regency is possible but speculative. | Falk says the biblical text gives the fourth year but does not explain the delay. He suggests possible reasons, such as consolidating the kingdom or a brief co-regency with David, but he marks these as conjectures because the passage itself does not give the rationale. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=4261" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:01</a> | What does Falk think of Ralph Hawkins's argument that the Exodus term may refer to squads rather than thousands? | He respects Hawkins and has no particular problem with the view, but says the passage could involve numerical exaggeration or mistranslation and cannot be pinned down with certainty. | Falk says Hawkins is a serious scholar and that reading the number in smaller military or social units is not objectionable to him. He keeps the answer cautious, noting that the text might involve exaggeration, a translation issue, or another numerical problem that cannot be resolved with certainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=4371" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:51</a> | How would Falk respond to the claim that Exodus co-opts Ramesside propaganda rather than recording history? | He says the objection assumes borrowing, whereas Joshua Berman's argument is about shared late Bronze Age literary forms and context, not direct literary dependence. | Falk says the stronger point is not that Exodus copied a specific Ramesside text. He frames Berman's argument as one about late Bronze Age literary and cultural conventions, meaning similar forms can place Exodus in the right milieu without requiring direct borrowing from Egyptian propaganda. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=4701" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:21</a> | Did David Rohl's chronology affect Kennedy's position, and what about ABR in general? | Falk does not think Rohl directly shaped Kennedy's position; he sees Kennedy as much more aligned with ABR's strict early-date and high-chronology approach. | Falk separates Kennedy from Rohl's revised chronology and instead associates him with ABR's conservative early-date framework. He says ABR tends to defend a high chronology and a strict 1446-style Exodus date, which creates different problems than Rohl's reconstruction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=4861" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:01</a> | How should the 450 years in Acts 13:20 be understood? | Falk says Acts 13 is textually and interpretively difficult, the starting point for the 450 years is debated, and it should not be used as a crux for an early-date argument. | Falk says Acts 13 has difficult wording and manuscript issues, with interpreters disagreeing over where the 450 years begin and what period they cover. Because the passage is ambiguous, he rejects using it as a decisive chronological proof for the early Exodus date. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=5415" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:15</a> | If Kennedy accepted the chronological alignment correction, would the archaeology match Hoffmeier's late-date view? | Falk says Kennedy did not contest the late Bronze destruction layer itself; the problem is trying to attach that destruction to Deborah rather than reading the strata reports correctly. | Falk says the relevant Late Bronze destruction is real and was not the main disputed point. The problem, in his view, is Kennedy's attempt to align that destruction with Deborah and Barak by mishandling the strata, whereas reading the reports correctly fits more naturally with the late-date framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=5668" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:28</a> | What does Falk think about reading Hebrew *suf* as a sea rather than reeds? | He says that confuses different periods of Hebrew; in the Pentateuch *suf* means reeds, and the sea reading appears later. | Falk says the argument confuses later Hebrew usage with Pentateuchal usage. In the Torah context, he says *suf* refers to reeds, while the word's later association with sea or end belongs to a different stage of Hebrew and should not be read back into Exodus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=6040" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:40</a> | Why not take the 480 years in 1 Kings 6:1 symbolically or approximately? | Falk says Kennedy's "exact almost" language is incoherent, and the early-date argument generally treats the number as exact even though the broader numerology and variants complicate that claim. | Falk says the early-date case usually depends on treating the 480 years as exact, so calling it almost exact weakens the argument. He also points to symbolic numerical patterns and textual complications as reasons the number cannot simply be pressed into a precise chronological anchor without further argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=6347" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:47</a> | How does Falk answer criticism that he seemed too harsh in the debate? | He says he intended to focus on arguments and be diplomatic, but shifted when he judged that incompetence and bad teaching needed to be exposed for viewers' benefit. | Falk says he went into the debate intending to stay focused on arguments and to be diplomatic. As the exchange developed, he concluded that Kennedy was making basic errors and teaching them publicly, so he believed viewers needed to see the incompetence rather than only a polite disagreement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=6559" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:19</a> | Could Judges 11 involve Ammonites working with Moabites against Israel? | Falk says cooperation is possible in theory, but the text gives no direct evidence; it presents a dispute between Jephthah and the Ammonites over land taken from the Amorites. | Falk allows that ancient groups could cooperate, but says Judges 11 itself does not state that Moabites are acting through or alongside the Ammonites. His reading stays with the text's presented dispute: Jephthah answers the Ammonites about land Israel took from the Amorites, not from Ammon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=6986" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:26</a> | How does the Judges 11 Ammonite-Amorite issue connect to the early-date versus late-date debate? | He explains that early daters add Jephthah's 300 years to an approximate date for Jephthah to reach around 1400 BC, making Judges 11 one of their standard proof texts. | Falk explains that Judges 11:26 is important because early-date advocates add Jephthah's 300 years to a date for Jephthah and use the result to support an Exodus around 1400 BC. He argues that this makes the passage a major proof text, so errors about Ammonites, Amorites, and the land claim matter directly to the chronology debate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=7277" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:17</a> | Does Hoffmeier make a similar plausibility argument for the Exodus? | Falk distinguishes Hoffmeier and Kitchen's inductive probability arguments from Kennedy's plausibility arguments, saying the former compile data with explanatory force while the latter lower the evidential bar. | Falk says Hoffmeier and Kitchen argue inductively from many pieces of data that collectively make the Exodus plausible in a stronger sense. He contrasts that with Kennedy's use of mere possibility, where a scenario is defended because it could fit rather than because the evidence positively points that way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=7583" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:23</a> | Are historical theories rarely strictly falsifiable because they can always be adjusted? | Falk says historical arguments can still be tested by challenging their premises, while an argument that is structured so it cannot be falsified has a serious evidential problem. | Falk agrees that historical arguments are often flexible, but says that does not make them immune to testing. A responsible theory has premises that can be examined and potentially rejected; if a claim is constructed so no evidence could count against it, that is an evidential defect. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=7756" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:16</a> | Should any Exodus-date view require evidence of upheaval during the relevant pharaoh's reign? | Falk says no, because the text does not require broad national upheaval; some plagues target specific agricultural cycles, and the firstborn deaths are hard to estimate archaeologically. | Falk says neither early nor late date should demand a clear empire-wide upheaval layer, because Exodus itself does not necessarily describe effects that archaeology would preserve in that way. He notes that some plagues concern crops or livestock and that estimating the archaeological footprint of firstborn deaths is especially difficult. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=8037" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:57</a> | Should the 600 chariots in Exodus be taken literally, and how many Egyptian chariots may have been lost? | He thinks Pharaoh gathered the elite chariot core plus nearby garrisons, estimating roughly three thousand chariots could have been involved, not infantry. | Falk reads the 600 as elite chariots rather than the entire force and suggests Pharaoh could have gathered additional chariots from nearby garrisons. He estimates that a larger force of about three thousand chariots is plausible in the scenario, while emphasizing chariots rather than infantry as the relevant military asset. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=8229" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:09</a> | Where were Egypt's main chariot garrisons, and were there chariots in the south toward Kush? | Falk names Per-Ramesses, Tjaru, Migdol, and Elim as key locations, and says the south was mainly infantry territory because the terrain was poor for chariots. | Falk identifies the eastern Delta and route toward Canaan as the natural chariot zone, naming Per-Ramesses, Tjaru, Migdol, and Elim. He says the southern theater toward Kush was not ideal chariot country, so Egyptian forces there would have relied much more on infantry. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=8447" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:47</a> | Has Falk lost friends over the late-date view, and is the debate as toxic as Genesis origins debates? | He says yes, he has lost friends and faced seminary consequences, and he considers the Exodus-date debate even more toxic. | Falk says the late-date position has cost him friendships and contributed to seminary difficulties. He describes the Exodus-date dispute as more toxic than origins debates because it functions as a loyalty marker in some conservative constituencies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=9028" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:28</a> | Why do some scholars seem willing to hint at both early-date and late-date positions rather than defend one openly? | He said the date of the Exodus is a publishing and constituency flashpoint, so some scholars hedge to avoid alienating readers or institutions. | Falk says scholars can feel pressure from publishers, institutions, and constituencies, especially when Exodus dating affects book sales or trust with conservative audiences. As a result, some write in a way that gestures toward both options rather than plainly defending the conclusion they think is strongest. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=9213" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:33</a> | Is Kenneth Kitchen like the egyptologist version of Ben Shapiro? | Falk says Kitchen is blunt and will directly say when he is unconvinced, then back it up with detailed critique. | Falk says Kitchen can be sharply direct and does not soften disagreement when he thinks an argument fails. The point of comparison is not style alone: Falk stresses that Kitchen backs blunt judgments with close critique and detailed knowledge. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=9617" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:17</a> | Was Falk at Liverpool for three years, and how did he move from philosophy and IT into Egyptology? | He says he worked in IT for nearly 20 years, used an employer education benefit to begin seminary, then moved through multiple graduate degrees into PhD work and research with Kenneth Kitchen. | Falk explains that he did not begin as an Egyptologist. He worked in IT for nearly two decades, started seminary through an employer education benefit, then moved from biblical and theological study into graduate work that eventually led to Liverpool, Egyptology, and work connected to Kenneth Kitchen. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=10713" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:33</a> | Where does Falk get the view that Genesis genealogical ages refer to clans or peoples? | He points to clan-based structures in the Old Testament, honorific transposition, abbreviated genealogies, and the broad use of clan categories in Israelite society. | Falk grounds the idea in how biblical genealogies can represent corporate clan structures rather than only individual lifespans. He mentions abbreviated genealogies, honorific transposition, and the importance of clans in Israelite society as reasons the Genesis ages may refer to peoples or lineages. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=11060" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:20</a> | How would Falk answer the claim that the Genesis ages systematically decrease after the flood? | He says later groups and notable figures can rise faster as societies accelerate and as the historical record becomes more expansive closer to the present. | Falk answers from the clan/people reading: decreasing ages can reflect how later peoples or notable groups rise and pass from prominence more quickly. He ties that to increasing social complexity and a fuller historical record as the narrative moves closer to better documented periods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=11400" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:00</a> | Was the Falk-Kennedy debate too short? | Falk says it was too short; McDowell had a hard stop and the debate only reached about half of Kennedy's planned agenda. | Falk says the event needed more time because Sean McDowell had a hard stop and the discussion only reached about half of Kennedy's prepared topics. He implies the format limited how thoroughly the chronological and archaeological arguments could be explored. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=11851" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:31</a> | Why does the absence of radiocarbon dates from ABR's el-Maqatir presentations raise a red flag? | Falk says Levantine radiocarbon dates tend to run older and work better for relative than absolute dating, and ABR would not benefit from dates that come out older than desired. | Falk says radiocarbon in the Levant often trends older and is more useful for relative placement than precise absolute dating. He treats the absence of published radiocarbon support as suspicious because dates that came out older than ABR wanted would not help their proposed identification or chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=12057" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:57</a> | What should someone do if they accidentally bought Titus Kennedy's *Unearthing the Bible*? | Falk says the pictures are good, but he considers the commentary shallow and substandard. | Falk's limited praise is for the visual material: he says the book has good pictures. He sharply criticizes the written commentary, describing it as shallow and not reliable enough to use as a serious guide to the subject. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=12188" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:08</a> | What are Kennedy's credentials, and is he fit to speak on the topic? | Falk says Kennedy is bona fide and credentialed, with a PhD from the University of South Africa, but he thinks Kennedy was not well trained. | Falk does not deny Kennedy's formal credentials, noting his PhD and bona fide status. His criticism is qualitative: he thinks Kennedy's training and performance show weaknesses, especially in handling Egyptological and archaeological evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=12526" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:46</a> | Can Falk give an example of how Sean McDowell's edited version made Kennedy look worse? | He says some freeze-and-recovery context was removed, leaving Kennedy appearing to stare silently before moving to the next subject. | Falk says one edited segment removed context around a technical freeze or recovery, making Kennedy appear to sit silently before changing topics. He presents this as an example where the shortened version could make Kennedy look worse than the live interaction did. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=13303" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:41:43</a> | Is a Sinai Peninsula location for Mount Sinai a problem because it lies near Egyptian sites like Serabit el-Khadim and Wadi Feiran? | Falk says Serabit el-Khadim was seasonally occupied, mining-focused, and separated from likely Israelite locations, so it does not create the danger assumed by the question. | Falk says Serabit el-Khadim was not a dense permanent Egyptian presence but a seasonal mining site, with activity tied to specific expeditions. Because likely Israelite routes or camps would be separated from that setting, he does not think the site's existence makes a Sinai Peninsula location implausible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=13829" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:29</a> | If Egyptians and Israelites had interacted militarily near Sinai, would the Israelites have recorded it? | Falk says yes, and therefore the burden of proof is on anyone claiming such a conflict occurred. | Falk says a major clash with Egyptians near Sinai would be the kind of event the Israelite narrative should mention. Since the text does not record such an encounter, he says the person proposing one must provide evidence rather than treating it as an assumed problem. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=14022" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:42</a> | Is a second Falk-Kennedy debate likely? | Falk doubts it will happen, but says he would be willing and was not intimidated by Kennedy. | Falk says he would be willing to debate again and was not intimidated by the first exchange. Even so, he doubts Kennedy or the organizers would pursue another event, given how the first debate unfolded. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=14863" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:07:43</a> | Is Falk a biblical scholar, or only an egyptologist? | He says his PhD is in Egyptology, but his master's work and specialization in Exodus and the Pentateuch give him sufficient biblical-scholarship expertise in that area. | Falk says his formal doctorate is in Egyptology, but he also has master's-level biblical training and focuses on Exodus and Pentateuchal questions. He presents that combination as enough to speak competently on the biblical material most relevant to his specialization. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=15216" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:13:36</a> | Was Falk still mad about the Deuteronomy 32 question that led to the Kip Davis controversy? | He says he was not personally upset with the questioner, and the later criticism grew from opponents watching his Q&A after prior critiques of Josh Bowen. | Falk says he did not resent the person who asked the Deuteronomy 32 question. He frames the later controversy as part of a broader chain of online criticism, where people who disliked his critiques of Josh Bowen watched the Q&A and used that exchange against him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=17953" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:59:13</a> | Is James Hoffmeier working on a new edition of *Israel in Egypt*? | Falk says Hoffmeier told him he was working on it, but that such plans should be taken with caution until they actually appear. | Falk says Hoffmeier personally told him he was working on a new edition of *Israel in Egypt*. He cautions that such statements often mean an intention rather than a guaranteed publication, so the claim should be held tentatively until a new edition actually appears. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=18052" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:00:52</a> | Is Hoffmeier's essay in *Five Views on the Exodus* his latest defense of the Exodus? | Falk agrees it is Hoffmeier's latest engagement, but wishes the volume had used younger scholars more closely engaged with current argumentation. | Falk agrees that Hoffmeier's contribution to *Five Views on the Exodus* is his latest summarized defense. His criticism is editorial: he wishes some views had been represented by younger scholars more active in the current state of the arguments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=18180" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:03:00</a> | Would Ronald Hendel be the strongest representative of his view in *Five Views on the Exodus*? | Falk thinks Hendel is probably the most erudite representative of that view and is not easy to replace with a younger scholar. | Falk says Hendel is probably the most erudite representative for his view, even if some other contributors might have had younger alternatives. He does not see an obvious replacement who would bring the same level of command to that position. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=18554" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:09:14</a> | How should Christians answer the Muslim claim that Exodus 5 describes a pilgrimage to Mecca? | Falk says the text is about a feast in the wilderness, not a Hajj, and the travel time to Mecca does not fit the three-day framework. | Falk says Exodus 5 speaks of a feast or sacrifice in the wilderness, not a Hajj to Mecca. He adds that Egyptian texts know temporary religious trips into the wilderness, and that the three-day framework cannot cover travel across Sinai and Arabia to Mecca. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=18780" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:13:00</a> | What does Falk think of Barry Beitzel being used by *Patterns of Evidence*? | He says Beitzel is a brilliant geographer and a friend, but he dislikes seeing a long-retired scholar used in that production context. | Falk speaks warmly of Beitzel as a friend, mentor, and brilliant geographer. His objection is to *Patterns of Evidence* using a scholar who has been retired for many years in a way Falk regards as exploitative or unfair to Beitzel's current situation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/3frfGREytOQ?t=18950" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:15:50</a> | Was there a wood shortage in Egypt during the late date, as IP mentions in his updated Exodus video? | Falk says yes; evidence appears from the end of Dynasty 20 into Dynasty 21, including coffin reuse and recycling during Dynasty 21. | Falk says the evidence for wood shortage appears especially from the end of Dynasty 20 into Dynasty 21. He points to the reuse and recycling of coffins as a sign that wood had become scarce, while also noting continued harvesting from local sources before that period. |
